nativeRange.habitat: Shallow, heavily shaded blackwater streams, forest pools, and peat swamps on Bangka Island; water stained dark brown by tannins from decomposing leaf litter and submerged vegetation; near-zero hardness, very low conductivity

decorPreferences: dense leaf litter (Indian almond/Catappa leaves) to lower pH and stain water
driftwood and bogwood to leach tannins
caves and hollow structures for spawning and retreat
fine-leaved or floating plants (Java moss, Salvinia, hornwort)
dim or heavily filtered lighting — shade with floating plants
dark substrate (fine black sand or peat)
peat filtration or blackwater extract to achieve pH 4.0–5.5

diet.notes: Micro-predator; in the wild feeds on tiny invertebrates, micro-crustaceans, and zooplankton. In captivity requires live or frozen micro-foods: micro worms, Daphnia, baby brine shrimp (Artemia nauplii), Cyclops, Grindal worms, and similar. Rarely accepts dry foods; some individuals can be conditioned onto frozen Cyclops or finely ground flake but live food remains essential for long-term health and breeding conditioning. Feed small amounts twice daily.

goodWith: conspecifics (kept as pairs or small groups in well-planted tanks)
other Parosphromenus species in species-only setups
small peaceful blackwater fish of similar size (Boraras spp., Sundadanio spp.)
small Otocinclus in matched water chemistry
avoidWith: larger or boisterous fish (will be outcompeted or bullied)
hard-water community fish incompatible with pH 4–5.5
any fish requiring neutral to alkaline water

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase photo gallery for Parosphromenus deissneri (species ID 11914). Check for CC-licensed contributor images; FishBase images vary by contributor.
Wikimedia Commons category for P. deissneri; verify image quality and licensing on access.

summary: A tiny (3–4 cm) IUCN Critically Endangered blackwater specialist endemic to Bangka Island, Indonesia. One of the most demanding freshwater aquarium fish: requires pH 4.0–5.5, near-zero hardness, tannin-stained water, and live micro-foods. True P. deissneri is rarely in trade; most sold fish are P. parvulus or other congeners. Conservation hobby breeding coordinated by the Parosphromenus Project.
careNotes: The Licorice Gourami (Parosphromenus deissneri) is widely regarded as one of the most demanding freshwater fish in the hobby. It is a strict blackwater specialist native to shallow peat-swamp streams on Bangka Island, Indonesia, where water is extremely soft (near-zero GH/KH), highly acidic (pH 4.0–5.5), and darkly tannin-stained from decomposing vegetation. Replicating this chemistry is non-negotiable: fish kept above pH 5.5 or in water with any measurable hardness typically decline and die within months. Use RO or rain water buffered only with peat, Indian almond (Catappa) leaves, and driftwood; never use tap water without full demineralisation in hard-water areas. Tanks should be heavily decorated with leaf litter, driftwood, caves, and dense fine-leaved plants with very dim lighting. A dedicated species tank or a carefully chosen blackwater nano community is strongly recommended — these fish are easily outcompeted for food and stressed by larger or boisterous tankmates. Feed exclusively on live or frozen micro-foods (micro worms, Daphnia, baby brine shrimp nauplii, Cyclops); dry foods are rarely accepted. Water temperature 24–26°C is optimal; slow, gentle filtration (sponge filter) is preferred as the species inhabits near-still waters and is sensitive to strong current. A critically important caveat for hobbyists: the vast majority of fish sold in trade as ‘P. deissneri’ or ‘Licorice Gourami’ are actually other Parosphromenus species — most commonly P. parvulus, P. linkei, P. nagyi, or undescribed congeners. The genus contains 20+ described species and accurate identification requires close examination of fin patterning, coloration, and geographic provenance. Hobbyists are strongly encouraged to source fish from specialist breeders participating in conservation programmes (see Parosphromenus Project: parosphromenus-project.org) rather than from general trade. IUCN Red List status: Critically Endangered (P. deissneri sensu stricto), driven by peat-swamp habitat destruction on Bangka Island from palm oil and tin mining.
breedingNotes: Cave spawner; males establish and defend a spawning cave (coconut shell half, PVC tube, or similar hollow structure). Courtship involves intense colour displays by the male, who develops vivid blue-black banding with iridescent blue fin markings. After spawning, the male guards and fans the egg cluster; he may be a mouthbrooder in some congeners but P. deissneri is primarily a cave/bubble-nest guarder. Fry are tiny and require infusoria or micro-worms as first foods before advancing to freshly hatched Artemia nauplii. Breeding success requires pH below 5.5, near-zero hardness, and excellent micro-food availability. Breeding in captivity is considered an important conservation act given wild habitat loss; surplus fry should be shared with other hobbyists through the Parosphromenus Project network.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Parosphromenus deissneri
primary.notes: Primary source for water parameters (pH 4.0–5.5, temperature 22–28°C, near-zero hardness), habitat description (Bangka Island blackwater peat swamps), diet (live micro-foods), care requirements, and breeding notes. Cross-referenced with FishBase and Parosphromenus Project for taxonomy, IUCN status, and trade misidentification notes. MERGE CANDIDATE: fw-fish-397 'Liquorice Gourami' (Parosphromenus spp.) covers the same genus. Recommend keeping both entries: fw-fish-395 as the species-level entry for P. deissneri sensu stricto; fw-fish-397 repurposed as a Parosphromenus spp. genus-group/care guide entry covering the broader 20+ species complex and trade misidentification. If merged, alsoKnownAs and careNotes of fw-fish-395 should absorb genus-level trade variation notes.

additional.name: FishBase — Parosphromenus deissneri
additional.notes: Taxonomy (Osphronemidae, Anabantiformes), adult size (to ~3.5 cm), native range (Bangka Island, Sumatra region, Indonesia), and IUCN Critically Endangered status confirmed. Distribution map cross-checked against nativeRange entry.

additional.name: Parosphromenus Project
additional.notes: Specialist conservation-breeding organisation coordinating captive populations of all Parosphromenus species. Source for trade misidentification prevalence (most ‘P. deissneri’ in trade are P. parvulus, P. linkei, or other congeners), genus species count (20+ described species), IUCN status, and conservation breeding guidance. Key reference for hobbyistAdvisory and careNotes.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Licorice Gourami care
additional.notes: UK hobbyist trade coverage of Parosphromenus spp.; water chemistry requirements (pH 4–5, very soft), feeding (live micro-foods required), tank setup, and specialist-only recommendation corroborated. Notes widespread misidentification of trade fish.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Gourami Care Overview
additional.notes: General gourami care reference. No dedicated P. deissneri page; Aquarium Co-Op does not carry or document specialist blackwater gouramis at the species level. Cited for completeness per source priority list; specific P. deissneri parameters sourced from SeriouslyFish and Parosphromenus Project.

fish.breedingNotes: Cave spawner with paternal egg guarding; male defends hollow spawning site and fans eggs. Requires pH below 5.5, near-zero hardness, and abundant live micro-foods for conditioning and fry feeding. Fry require infusoria then micro worms before Artemia nauplii. Considered advanced due to strict water chemistry and micro-food requirements. Conservation breeding strongly encouraged.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Strict blackwater specialist demanding pH 4.0–5.5 and tea-stained tannin water with near-zero hardness. IUCN Critically Endangered narrow Bangka Island endemic; trade fish often misidentified Parosphromenus species. Specialist conservation breeding hobby.
